472	\begin{lemma}\leanok
473	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
474	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
476	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
477	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
478	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
479	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
480	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
481	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
482	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
483	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
484	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
485	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
486	% is hand-proved per a.
487	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
488	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
489	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
490	  \[
491	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
492	    \;\cong\;
493	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
494	  \]
495	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
496	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
497	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
498	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
499	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
500	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
501	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
502	
503	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
504	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
505	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
506	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
507	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
508	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
509	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
510	\end{lemma}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
514	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
515	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
516	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
517	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
518	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
519	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
520	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
521	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
522	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
523	
524	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
526	  \[
527	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
528	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
529	  \]
530	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
531	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
532	
533	  \medskip
534	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
535	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
536	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
537	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
541	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
542	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
543	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
544	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
545	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
546	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
547	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
548	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
549	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
550	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
551	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
552	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
553	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
554	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
555	  an isomorphism.
556	
557	  \medskip
558	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
559	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
560	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
561	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
562	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
563	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
564	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
565	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
566	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
567	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
569	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
570	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
571	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
572	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
573	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
574	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
575	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
576	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
578	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
580	  movements.
581	
582	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
583	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
584	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
585	  \[
586	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
587	      \;\cong\;
588	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
589	  \]
590	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
591	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
592	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
593	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
594	
595	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
596	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
597	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
599	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
601	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
602	  \[
603	    \begin{array}{ccc}
604	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
605	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
606	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
607	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
608	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
609	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
610	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
611	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
612	    \end{array}
613	  \]
614	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
615	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
616	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
617	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
618	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
619	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
620	  \(\widetilde M\).
621	
622	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
623	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
624	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
625	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
626	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
627	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
629	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
630	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
631	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
632	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
633	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
634	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
635	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
636	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
637	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
638	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
639	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
640	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
641	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
642	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
643	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
644	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
645	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
646	
647	  \medskip
648	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
649	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
650	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
651	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
652	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
653	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
654	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
655	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
656	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
657	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
658	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
659	  naturality square for the inclusion
660	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
661	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
662	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
663	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
664	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
665	
666	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
667	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
668	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
669	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
670	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
671	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
672	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
673	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
674	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
675	
676	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
677	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
678	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
679	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
680	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
681	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
682	  % bump.
683	  \medskip
684	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
685	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
686	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
687	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
688	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
689	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
690	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
691	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
692	  needed for the affine lane.
693	\end{proof}
694	
695	\begin{remark}
696	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
697	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
698	  \begin{enumerate}
699	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
700	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
701	      \(R\)-module of sections of
702	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
703	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
704	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
705	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
706	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
707	      closed under isomorphism; hence
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
709	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
710	      needed for the affine lane.
711	  \end{enumerate}
712	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
713	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
714	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
715	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
716	\end{remark}

1267	/-- **(iii-1) Unit expansion: inverting the comp-coherence.** For composable `a : X₁ ⟶ X₂`,
1268	`b : X₂ ⟶ X₃`, a module `N` on `X₃`, and `g' = a ≫ b`, the bare `(g')`-unit expands as the
1269	four-factor composite `η^b ≫ b_*(η^a) ≫ pushforwardComp(a,b).hom ≫ g'_*(pullbackComp(a,b).hom)`.

1271	identity with `g'_*(pullbackComp.hom)` collapses the trailing `pullbackComp.inv` (functoriality of
1272	`g'_*` on `inv ≫ hom = id`). See blueprint `lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand`. -/
1273	theorem base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand {X₁ X₂ X₃ : Scheme.{u}}
1274	    (a : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (b : X₂ ⟶ X₃) (N : X₃.Modules) :
1275	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (a ≫ b)).unit.app N
1276	      = ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction b).unit.app N ≫
1277	          (Scheme.Modules.pushforward b).map
1278	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction a).unit.app
1279	              ((Scheme.Modules.pullback b).obj N)) ≫
1280	          (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a b).hom.app
1281	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback b).obj N))) ≫
1282	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (a ≫ b)).map
1283	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a b).hom.app N) := by
1284	  rw [pullbackPushforward_unit_comp a b N]
1285	  -- The two trailing `(a ≫ b)_*`-images cancel (`pullbackComp.inv ≫ pullbackComp.hom = 𝟙`), but a
1286	  -- spurious `X.Modules` instance diamond under single-file elaboration makes `rw [Category.assoc]`
1287	  -- / `rw [hI]` / `simp only [Iso.inv_hom_id_app]` misfire on the goal's locked composition. We
1288	  -- prove the cancellation on FRESH terms (where the rewrites match) and discharge the goal in
1289	  -- term mode (`exact`), which checks defeq and tolerates the diamond.
1290	  have hI : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (a ≫ b)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a b).inv.app N) ≫
1291	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (a ≫ b)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a b).hom.app N)
1292	      = 𝟙 _ := by
1293	    rw [← Functor.map_comp, Iso.inv_hom_id_app, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]
1294	  have hfin := (congrArg (fun z =>
1295	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (a ≫ b)).unit.app N ≫ z) hI).trans
1296	    (Category.comp_id _)

1486	  -- `(Spec φ)_* ⋙ Γ_R` image) must cancel the codomain read's `unit_iso.symm = (asIso η^{e.hom}).symm`
1487	  -- (living in the `Γ_R' → gammaPushforwardIso ψ → restrictScalars ψ` image), and factor 3
1488	  -- (`pullbackComp(e,inclA).hom`) must cancel `iso_g`'s `pullbackComp(e,inclA).symm` ACROSS that same
1489	  -- layer transport. These two factors sit in DIFFERENT functor images, so the cancellation is NOT
1490	  -- adjacency bookkeeping — it requires the naturality of `gammaPushforwardIso ψ` bridging the
1491	  -- `Spec R`-reading of `(Spec φ)_*` to the `Spec R'`-reading used by the codomain read. That bridging
1492	  -- naturality lemma is the missing ingredient (see informal/base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs.md);
1493	  -- it is the same `conjugateEquiv`/mate coherence that closed Seam 1 (`base_change_mate_unit_value`),
1494	  -- now needed at the reindex layer. Direct-on-sections term-mode splicing bottoms out here.
1495	  sorry
1496	
1497	-- The `exact`-onto-`…_legs` reduction unfolds two large change-of-rings dictionaries and certifies
1498	-- a proof-irrelevant defeq between the concrete and variable-legs codomain reads.
1499	set_option maxHeartbeats 1600000 in
1500	/-- **Seam 2: the pushforward pseudofunctor reindex of the inner comparison.** In the generic
1501	pullback square with legs `g' = pullback.fst`, `f' = pullback.snd`, write `θ_in` for the inner
1502	composite built from the `(g')`-unit and the three pushforward pseudofunctor coherences
1503	(`pushforwardComp` twice, `pushforwardCongr` once) that appear inside `pushforwardBaseChangeMap`
1504	before the `(g^* ⊣ g_*)`-transpose. Read on global sections over `Spec R` through the
1505	`Γ`-pushforward dictionaries (`gammaPushforwardTildeIso` / `gammaPushforwardIso`), with the
1506	codomain pinned by `base_change_mate_codomain_read`, the map `θ_in` is the canonical `R`-linear map
1507	`ρ : m ↦ (1 ⊗ 1) ⊗ m` (`base_change_mate_inner_value`), i.e. `restrictScalars ψ` of the
1508	codomain-read transport of Seam 1's unit value. See blueprint `lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex`. -/
1509	theorem base_change_mate_fstar_reindex {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}}

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2025 Joël Riou. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Joël Riou
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Adjunction.Mates
9	
10	/-!
11	# Compatibilities for left adjoints from compatibilities satisfied by right adjoints
12	
13	In this file, given isomorphisms between compositions of right adjoint functors,
14	we obtain isomorphisms between the corresponding compositions of the left adjoint functors,
15	and show that the left adjoint functors satisfy properties similar to the left/right
16	unitality and the associativity of pseudofunctors if the right adjoint functors
17	satisfy the corresponding properties.
18	
19	This is used in `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Pullback` to study
20	the behaviour with respect to composition of the pullback functors on presheaves
21	of modules, by reducing these definitions and properties to the (obvious) case of the
22	pushforward functors. Similar results are obtained for sheaves of modules
23	in `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackContinuous`.
24	
25	-/
26	
27	@[expose] public section
28	
29	namespace CategoryTheory
30	
31	variable {C₀ C₁ C₂ C₃ : Type*} [Category* C₀] [Category* C₁] [Category* C₂] [Category* C₃]
32	
33	open Functor
34	
35	namespace Adjunction
36	
37	section
38	
39	variable {F : C₀ ⥤ C₀} {G : C₀ ⥤ C₀} (adj : F ⊣ G) (e : G ≅ 𝟭 C₀)
40	
41	/-- If a right adjoint functor is isomorphic to the identity functor,
42	so is the left adjoint. -/
43	@[simps! -isSimp]
44	def leftAdjointIdIso {F : C₀ ⥤ C₀} {G : C₀ ⥤ C₀} (adj : F ⊣ G) (e : G ≅ 𝟭 C₀) :
45	    F ≅ 𝟭 C₀ := (conjugateIsoEquiv .id adj).symm e.symm
46	
47	@[simp]
48	lemma conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointIdIso_hom :
49	    conjugateEquiv .id adj (leftAdjointIdIso adj e).hom = e.inv := by
50	  simp [leftAdjointIdIso]
51	
52	end
53	
54	section
55	
56	variable {F₀₁ : C₀ ⥤ C₁} {F₁₂ : C₁ ⥤ C₂} {F₀₂ : C₀ ⥤ C₂}
57	  {G₁₀ : C₁ ⥤ C₀} {G₂₁ : C₂ ⥤ C₁} {G₂₀ : C₂ ⥤ C₀}
58	  (adj₀₁ : F₀₁ ⊣ G₁₀) (adj₁₂ : F₁₂ ⊣ G₂₁) (adj₀₂ : F₀₂ ⊣ G₂₀)
59	
60	/-- A natural transformation `G₂₀ ⟶ G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀` involving right adjoint functors
61	induces a natural transformation `F₀₁ ⋙ F₁₂ ⟶ F₀₂` between the corresponding
62	left adjoint functors. -/
63	@[simps! -isSimp]
64	def leftAdjointCompNatTrans (τ₀₁₂ : G₂₀ ⟶ G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀) :
65	    F₀₁ ⋙ F₁₂ ⟶ F₀₂ :=
66	  (conjugateEquiv adj₀₂ (adj₀₁.comp adj₁₂)).symm τ₀₁₂
67	
68	/-- A natural isomorphism `G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₂₀` involving right adjoint functors
69	induces a natural isomorphism `F₀₁ ⋙ F₁₂ ≅ F₀₂` between the corresponding
70	left adjoint functors. -/
71	@[simps! -isSimp]
72	def leftAdjointCompIso (e₀₁₂ : G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₂₀) :
73	    F₀₁ ⋙ F₁₂ ≅ F₀₂ :=
74	  (conjugateIsoEquiv adj₀₂ (adj₀₁.comp adj₁₂)).symm e₀₁₂.symm
75	
76	lemma leftAdjointCompIso_hom (e₀₁₂ : G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₂₀) :
77	    (leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ e₀₁₂).hom =
78	      leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ e₀₁₂.inv :=
79	  rfl
80	
81	@[simp]
82	lemma conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv (e₀₁₂ : G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₂₀) :
83	    conjugateEquiv (adj₀₁.comp adj₁₂) adj₀₂
84	      (leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ e₀₁₂).inv = e₀₁₂.hom := by
85	  dsimp only [leftAdjointCompIso]
86	  simp
87	
88	end
89	
90	lemma leftAdjointCompIso_comp_id
91	    {F₀₁ : C₀ ⥤ C₁} {F₁₁' : C₁ ⥤ C₁} {G₁₀ : C₁ ⥤ C₀} {G₁'₁ : C₁ ⥤ C₁}
92	    (adj₀₁ : F₀₁ ⊣ G₁₀) (adj₁₁' : F₁₁' ⊣ G₁'₁)
93	    (e₀₁₁' : G₁'₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₁₀) (e₁'₁ : G₁'₁ ≅ 𝟭 _)
94	    (h : e₀₁₁' = isoWhiskerRight e₁'₁ G₁₀ ≪≫ leftUnitor G₁₀) :
95	    leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₁ adj₁₁' adj₀₁ e₀₁₁' =
96	      isoWhiskerLeft _ (leftAdjointIdIso adj₁₁' e₁'₁) ≪≫ rightUnitor F₀₁ := by
97	  subst h
98	  ext X₀
99	  simp [leftAdjointCompIso_hom_app, leftAdjointIdIso_hom_app,
100	    ← Functor.map_comp_assoc, -Functor.map_comp]
101	
102	lemma leftAdjointCompIso_id_comp
103	    {F₀₀' : C₀ ⥤ C₀} {F₀'₁ : C₀ ⥤ C₁} {G₀'₀ : C₀ ⥤ C₀} {G₁₀' : C₁ ⥤ C₀}
104	    (adj₀₀' : F₀₀' ⊣ G₀'₀) (adj₀'₁ : F₀'₁ ⊣ G₁₀')
105	    (e₀₀'₁ : G₁₀' ⋙ G₀'₀ ≅ G₁₀') (e₀'₀ : G₀'₀ ≅ 𝟭 _)
106	    (h : e₀₀'₁ = isoWhiskerLeft G₁₀' e₀'₀ ≪≫ rightUnitor G₁₀') :
107	    leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₀' adj₀'₁ adj₀'₁ e₀₀'₁ =
108	      isoWhiskerRight (leftAdjointIdIso adj₀₀' e₀'₀) F₀'₁ ≪≫ leftUnitor F₀'₁ := by
109	  subst h
110	  ext X₀
111	  have h₁ := congr_map F₀'₁ (adj₀₀'.counit.naturality (adj₀'₁.unit.app X₀))
112	  have h₂ := congr_map (F₀₀' ⋙ F₀'₁) (e₀'₀.inv.naturality (adj₀'₁.unit.app X₀))
113	  simp only [id_obj, comp_obj, Functor.id_map, Functor.comp_map, Functor.map_comp] at h₁ h₂
114	  simp [leftAdjointCompIso_hom_app, leftAdjointIdIso_hom_app,
115	    reassoc_of% h₂, reassoc_of% h₁]
116	
117	section
118	
119	variable
120	  {F₀₁ : C₀ ⥤ C₁} {F₁₂ : C₁ ⥤ C₂} {F₂₃ : C₂ ⥤ C₃} {F₀₂ : C₀ ⥤ C₂} {F₁₃ : C₁ ⥤ C₃} {F₀₃ : C₀ ⥤ C₃}
121	  {G₁₀ : C₁ ⥤ C₀} {G₂₁ : C₂ ⥤ C₁} {G₃₂ : C₃ ⥤ C₂} {G₂₀ : C₂ ⥤ C₀} {G₃₁ : C₃ ⥤ C₁} {G₃₀ : C₃ ⥤ C₀}
122	  (adj₀₁ : F₀₁ ⊣ G₁₀) (adj₁₂ : F₁₂ ⊣ G₂₁) (adj₂₃ : F₂₃ ⊣ G₃₂) (adj₀₂ : F₀₂ ⊣ G₂₀)
123	  (adj₁₃ : F₁₃ ⊣ G₃₁) (adj₀₃ : F₀₃ ⊣ G₃₀)
124	
125	section
126	
127	variable (τ₀₁₂ : G₂₀ ⟶ G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀) (τ₁₂₃ : G₃₁ ⟶ G₃₂ ⋙ G₂₁)
128	  (τ₀₁₃ : G₃₀ ⟶ G₃₁ ⋙ G₁₀) (τ₀₂₃ : G₃₀ ⟶ G₃₂ ⋙ G₂₀)
129	
130	lemma leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₁₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm :
131	    whiskerLeft _ (leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₁₂ adj₂₃ adj₁₃ τ₁₂₃) ≫
132	      leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₁ adj₁₃ adj₀₃ τ₀₁₃ =
133	    (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₁.comp (adj₁₂.comp adj₂₃))).symm
134	      (τ₀₁₃ ≫ whiskerRight τ₁₂₃ G₁₀) := by
135	  obtain ⟨τ₁₂₃, rfl⟩ := (conjugateEquiv adj₁₃ (adj₁₂.comp adj₂₃)).surjective τ₁₂₃
136	  obtain ⟨τ₀₁₃, rfl⟩ := (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₁.comp adj₁₃)).surjective τ₀₁₃
137	  apply (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₁.comp (adj₁₂.comp adj₂₃))).injective
138	  simp [leftAdjointCompNatTrans, ← conjugateEquiv_whiskerLeft _ _ adj₀₁]
139	
140	lemma leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₂₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm :
141	    (associator _ _ _).inv ≫
142	      whiskerRight (leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ τ₀₁₂) F₂₃ ≫
143	          leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₂ adj₂₃ adj₀₃ τ₀₂₃ =
144	    (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₁.comp (adj₁₂.comp adj₂₃))).symm
145	      (τ₀₂₃ ≫ whiskerLeft G₃₂ τ₀₁₂ ≫ (associator _ _ _).inv) := by
146	  obtain ⟨τ₀₁₂, rfl⟩ := (conjugateEquiv adj₀₂ (adj₀₁.comp adj₁₂)).surjective τ₀₁₂
147	  obtain ⟨τ₀₂₃, rfl⟩ := (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₂.comp adj₂₃)).surjective τ₀₂₃
148	  apply (conjugateEquiv adj₀₃ (adj₀₁.comp (adj₁₂.comp adj₂₃))).injective
149	  simp only [Equiv.apply_symm_apply, Equiv.symm_apply_apply, leftAdjointCompNatTrans]
150	  rw [← cancel_mono (associator G₃₂ G₂₁ G₁₀).hom, Category.assoc, Category.assoc,
151	    Iso.inv_hom_id, Category.comp_id, ← conjugateEquiv_associator_hom adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₂₃,
152	    ← conjugateEquiv_whiskerRight _ _ adj₂₃, conjugateEquiv_comp, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc,
153	    conjugateEquiv_comp]
154	
155	lemma leftAdjointCompNatTrans_assoc
156	    (h : τ₀₂₃ ≫ whiskerLeft G₃₂ τ₀₁₂ =
157	      τ₀₁₃ ≫ whiskerRight τ₁₂₃ G₁₀ ≫ (associator _ _ _).hom) :
158	    whiskerLeft _ (leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₁₂ adj₂₃ adj₁₃ τ₁₂₃) ≫
159	        leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₁ adj₁₃ adj₀₃ τ₀₁₃ =
160	      (associator _ _ _).inv ≫
161	        whiskerRight (leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ τ₀₁₂) F₂₃ ≫
162	          leftAdjointCompNatTrans adj₀₂ adj₂₃ adj₀₃ τ₀₂₃ := by
163	  simp [leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₁₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm,
164	    leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₂₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm, reassoc_of% h]
165	
166	end
167	
168	lemma leftAdjointCompIso_assoc
169	    (e₀₁₂ : G₂₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₂₀) (e₁₂₃ : G₃₂ ⋙ G₂₁ ≅ G₃₁)
170	    (e₀₁₃ : G₃₁ ⋙ G₁₀ ≅ G₃₀) (e₀₂₃ : G₃₂ ⋙ G₂₀ ≅ G₃₀)
171	    (h : isoWhiskerLeft G₃₂ e₀₁₂ ≪≫ e₀₂₃ =
172	      (associator _ _ _).symm ≪≫ isoWhiskerRight e₁₂₃ _ ≪≫ e₀₁₃) :
173	    isoWhiskerLeft _ (leftAdjointCompIso adj₁₂ adj₂₃ adj₁₃ e₁₂₃) ≪≫
174	        leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₁ adj₁₃ adj₀₃ e₀₁₃ =
175	      (associator _ _ _).symm ≪≫
176	        isoWhiskerRight (leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₁ adj₁₂ adj₀₂ e₀₁₂) F₂₃ ≪≫
177	          leftAdjointCompIso adj₀₂ adj₂₃ adj₀₃ e₀₂₃ := by

1001	                ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars inclA.hom).obj

/-! ### Seam 2, conjugate-route foundation (iter-035 pivot)

The iter-034 direct-on-sections route for `base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs` bottoms out at the
cross-layer naturality of `gammaPushforwardIso ψ` under the `X.Modules` instance diamond. The pivot
(analogies/fbc-mate-reencode.md) re-encodes the comparison object natively in the composite-adjunction
conjugate calculus of `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Adjunction.CompositionIso`, where the diamond never
forms because the lock-prone objects become metavariables via `conjugateEquiv.surjective`/`.injective`.
The first building block is the identification of the project's pseudofunctor coherence
`pullbackComp f g` with the abstract `leftAdjointCompIso` of the pushforward coherence `pushforwardComp f g`. -/

/-- **(conj-0) `pullbackComp` is the `leftAdjointCompIso` of `pushforwardComp`.** The project's
pseudofunctor pullback-composition coherence `(pullbackComp f g).inv` agrees with the abstract
left-adjoint composition isomorphism `leftAdjointCompIso` built from the pushforward coherence
`pushforwardComp f g`. Both are characterised by the same image under
`conjugateEquiv (adjg.comp adjf) adj(f≫g)` — namely `(pushforwardComp f g).hom` — by the project's
`conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` and Mathlib's `conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv`; injectivity
of `conjugateEquiv` closes the identity. This is the Step-(i) foundation of the conjugate re-encoding
of `base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs`. -/
theorem pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f g).inv
      = (Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g)
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g))
          (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f g)).inv := by
  apply (conjugateEquiv ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).comp
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g))).injective
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv,
    Adjunction.conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv]

`Iso.inv_eq_inv` exists. Let me add the iso-level convenience lemma built on conj-0.

input.old_string:
rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv,
    Adjunction.conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv]

input.new_string:
rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv,
    Adjunction.conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv]

/-- **(conj-0′) Iso-level form of `pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv`.** Upgrades the
inverse-level identity to a full isomorphism equality `pullbackComp f g = leftAdjointCompIso …`, so
the conjugate re-encoding can rewrite `pullbackComp` for `leftAdjointCompIso` (and vice versa) freely
in the comparison object. Proved from the inv-level identity via `Iso.inv_eq_inv` + `Iso.ext`. -/
theorem pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
    Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f g
      = Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g)
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f)
          (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ g))
          (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f g) :=
  Iso.ext ((Iso.inv_eq_inv _ _).mp (pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv f g))

/-! ### Seam 2, step (ii): Γ-collapse of the transparent pushforward coherences

new_string:

input.content:
# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean — FBC-A (iter-034 prover, fine-grained)

Lane: **FBC-A conjugate-side re-encoding** of the `_legs` crux. Per the directive, the
direct-on-sections route (`..._link_distributeCollapse`/`_cancelEUnit`/…) is ABANDONED — its
term-mode splice bottoms at the cross-layer naturality of `gammaPushforwardIso ψ` under the
`X.Modules` instance diamond. This round laid the verified **Step-(i) foundation** of the conjugate
route (`analogies/fbc-mate-reencode.md`): identifying the project's pseudofunctor coherence
`pullbackComp` with the abstract `leftAdjointCompIso` of `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Adjunction.CompositionIso`.

## Architecture recap (where the sorries live)

The Seam-3 chain is now ONE genuine crux deep. Verified-clean already in-file:
- **Seam 1** `base_change_mate_unit_value` — closed via `unit_conjugateEquiv_symm` (conjugate calculus).
- **Seam 3 counit** `base_change_mate_gstar_counit_transport` — closed via `conjugateEquiv_counit_symm`.
- **Seam 3 generator close** `base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close` — closed (one-generator `ext`).
- **eCancel cancellations** `base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_{eUnit,pushforwardComp,pullbackComp}` — closed.

The 4 remaining `sorry`s (post-edit line numbers):
1. `base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs` @1425 — **THE crux** (Seam-2 reindex `Γ_R(θ_in)=ρ`).
2. `base_change_mate_gstar_transpose` @1788 — cascades off #1 (its inner reindex must reuse #1).
3. affine `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` @2061 — out of scope this round.
4. FBC-B target @2101 — superseded by `FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean` (out of scope).

Closing #1 cascades #2 (and `base_change_mate_fstar_reindex` + `base_change_mate_inner_value_eq`
become axiom-clean automatically), per the directive.

## Sentence decomposition of the conjugate route (recipe `analogies/fbc-mate-reencode.md`)

### Sentence (conj-0): "`pullbackComp f g` is the `leftAdjointCompIso` of `pushforwardComp f g`."
→ `pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` (inv-level) and `pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso` (iso-level).
- **Result:** RESOLVED — both **proved, axiom-clean** (verified: `propext/Classical.choice/Quot.sound`).
- Proof: both `(pullbackComp f g).inv` and `(leftAdjointCompIso adjg adjf adj(f≫g) (pushforwardComp f g)).inv`
  have the SAME image `(pushforwardComp f g).hom` under `conjugateEquiv (adjg.comp adjf) adj(f≫g)`
  (project `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` + Mathlib `conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv`);
  `conjugateEquiv.injective` closes the inv-level identity; `Iso.inv_eq_inv` + `Iso.ext` upgrades to
  the iso-level identity. This is the Step-(i) device the recipe asks for: the comparison object can
  now be rewritten natively in the `leftAdjointCompIso` calculus, where the conjugate-side discharge
  (`.surjective`→`.injective`→reassoc conjugate set) avoids the diamond entirely.
- **Confirmed available in pinned Mathlib:** `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/CompositionIso.lean`
  (`leftAdjointCompIso` @72, `conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` @82,
  `leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₂₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm` @140) — the recipe's "no new Mathlib" claim holds.

### Sentence (conj-1): "Re-cut `base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs` proof-free from `leftAdjointCompIso`."
- **Result:** PARTIAL / NOT ATTEMPTED IN-FILE (deliberately deferred).
- **Reason:** the existing `base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs` is consumed *definitionally* by the
  concrete `base_change_mate_fstar_reindex` (`exact base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs … hfst hsnd …`
  relies on the concrete `base_change_mate_codomain_read` being defeq to `_legs … hfst hsnd`). A naive
  re-cut risks breaking that green `exact`. The correct sequencing is to introduce the conjugate
  codomain read as a NEW def, prove `_legs` against it, then bridge the concrete read — a larger change
  than a few-tool-call fine-grained step admits, and the right scope for iter-035's "one more round."
- **Next step (handoff):** define `base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj` via
  `leftAdjointCompIso (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction e.hom) (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (Spec inclA)) …`
  using conj-0′ to relate it to the existing read, carrying NO `hfst/hsnd` data (legs are the explicit
  composite `e.hom ≫ Spec.map inclA`).

### Sentence (conj-2): "Discharge `_legs` on the conjugate side via `.surjective`/`.injective` + reassoc set."
- **Result:** sorry retained (blocker below). Template: `leftAdjointCompNatTrans₀₂₃_eq_conjugateEquiv_symm`
  (CompositionIso.lean:140) — `obtain ⟨τ, rfl⟩ := (conjugateEquiv …).surjective …` →
  `apply (conjugateEquiv …).injective` → close with `conjugateEquiv_comp`/`_symm_comp`/`_whiskerLeft`/
  `_whiskerRight`/`_associator_hom` (Mates.lean) + conj-0 + `gammaMap_pushforwardComp_*`.
- **Blocker:** depends on conj-1 (the re-cut codomain read) to put the whole `_legs` equation into a
  single `conjugateEquiv`-component shape. Until the comparison object lives natively in the
  `leftAdjointCompIso` calculus, the goal stays in `ModuleCat R` global-sections form where the diamond
  bites (the current @1425 sorry goal). conj-0/conj-0′ remove the representational obstacle; conj-1 is
  the remaining structural step.

## Summary
- **Sentences closed this round: 1/3** (conj-0, both inv- and iso-level forms = 2 new axiom-clean lemmas).
- **sorry count: 4 → 4** (unchanged; the 2 new lemmas are sorry-free additions, not sorry replacements).
- Open: conj-1 (codomain-read re-cut, deferred to avoid breaking the green concrete `exact`) and conj-2
  (`_legs` conjugate discharge, blocked on conj-1). `gstar_transpose` @1788 unchanged (cascades off `_legs`).

## Why I stopped
**Real progress:** 2/2 attempted conjugate-route foundation lemmas closed and verified axiom-clean —
`pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` and `pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso`. These are the
Step-(i) device the recipe identifies as the prerequisite for the entire conjugate re-encoding, and
they confirm the pinned Mathlib has the full `CompositionIso` calculus (the recipe's load-bearing
assumption). The build is GREEN (`lake build AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.FlatBaseChange` → 8318 jobs,
only the 4 pre-existing sorries + style warnings).

**Did NOT close `_legs`/`gstar_transpose`** — so the directive's **tripwire fires**: iter-035 should
re-break the conjugate route ONE more round (now with conj-0/conj-0′ in hand: do conj-1 = re-cut the
codomain read as a NEW `leftAdjointCompIso`-native def, then conj-2 = `_legs` via
`.surjective`/`.injective` per the CompositionIso.lean:140 template), then iter-036 escalates to the user.

**Partial conjugate progress left in-file** (as the tripwire requests): the new `Seam 2, conjugate-route
foundation` section (~@1163) with the two verified lemmas; the abandoned direct-on-sections body of
`base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs` is retained beneath its `sorry` per the no-bare-sorry rule.

**Deferred (not "too hard" — scoped):** conj-1 (codomain-read re-cut) is a definitional-compatibility
change touching the concrete `base_change_mate_fstar_reindex` `exact`; doing it safely needs a
new-def-then-bridge sequence larger than a fine-grained per-sentence step. conj-2 is blocked on conj-1.

## Blueprint markers (for review agent)
- `pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv` and `pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso` are new public
  helpers in the FBC-A conjugate-route foundation. They are bespoke project infrastructure (no external
  citation); the plan agent may wish to add `\lean{}` blocks under `chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange`
  (Seam-2 conjugate-route subsection) wired `\uses` into `lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs`.
  Both are ready for `\leanok` once blueprint blocks exist (sync_leanok will confirm).

## Blueprint dependency graph (leandag)

You can navigate the project's dependency graph read-only to find the
proven lemmas/defs your goal can lean on (with the exact `\lean{}`
names to apply) instead of re-deriving them. `archon` is on PATH;
`--json` prints parseable JSON to stdout (banner to stderr):

```
archon dag-query ancestors --node <blueprint-label-of-your-target>   # proven deps available to you
archon dag-query node      --node <label>                            # one declaration's status
```

It is a navigation aid only — the proof obligation and your blueprint
chapter remain the source of truth.

Active prover mode: **mathlib-build**

## Your goal

Build project-local Mathlib infrastructure axiom-clean, one step at a time, going **as far as possible** in this iteration. Your output consists of definitions and lemmas that compile without `sorry` and whose `#print axioms` shows only `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`. You stop only when you hit a genuine mathematical blocker — not because one step is hard.

## Invariant

**No `sorry` in your output.** Every declaration you add must be fully proved. If you cannot close the current step: try alternatives first, then either prove a smaller sub-step or leave the declaration absent. Never add a declaration with `sorry`.

## Workflow

### 1. Orient

- Read `PROGRESS.md` for the objective: what infrastructure is needed, and why.
- Read the relevant blueprint chapter. It often contains the mathematical path to the ingredient you're building.
- Read `task_pending.md` for prior dead ends. Do not repeat them.

### 2. Scout the Mathlib API

Before writing any Lean, search for what already exists:

- `lean_local_search` for related names.
- `lean_leansearch` with a description of the mathematical content.
- `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns.
- Look for near-misses: a lemma that covers a sub-case, an instance you can compose, a `simp` set that contains what you need.

Even if you think you know the API — search anyway. Signatures change, instances disappear.

### 3. Build bottom-up, step by step

Start from the deepest missing piece identified in your scouting. For each step:

1. **Draft the type signature** and check it typechecks via `lean_diagnostic_messages` or `lean_run_code` before attempting the body.
2. **Write the proof.** Use Mathlib lemmas you verified exist. If a sub-step is missing, recurse: prove that sub-step first.
3. **Verify axiom-cleanliness** immediately after the step compiles: `#print axioms MyLemma`. Any `sorryAx` in the ancestry means the step is not axiom-clean — do not proceed until it is gone.
4. **Continue to the next step** in the chain.

Push as far as you can. Each axiom-clean step you add is permanent progress.

### 4. When stuck on a step — try before stopping

Before declaring a step impossible:

1. **Try a different proof route.** Reformulate the statement, weaken to a special case you can prove, use a detour through an equivalent form.
2. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 3.
3. **Search more broadly.** The lemma you need might exist under a different name or in a namespace you haven't checked.
4. **Prove a strictly smaller sub-step** that is axiom-clean and genuinely useful — something that shrinks the remaining gap.

Only after exhausting these alternatives do you stop. **Comments describing an approach you haven't tried are not exhausted alternatives — attempt them first.**

### 5. Stopping

**Before stopping, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment, TODO, or log entry? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there adjacent declarations in the chain I could attempt with the infrastructure I just built?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before declaring the step impossible?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped? Attempt it — a failed attempt with a named error is better than a comment.

Stop only when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable." **Writing a comment about a possible approach counts as identifying it — which means you should attempt it.**

**You are fully authorized to make every mathematical and structural decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide which route to take" is never a valid stop reason — pick the most promising route, attempt it, document the outcome. The planner corrects route choices next iter; an idle session corrects nothing.

Stop when you have tried alternatives and cannot make further axiom-clean progress. Before stopping:

- Commit all axiom-clean steps you completed.
- Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with a precise handoff (see Logging below).
- Ensure the file compiles with no `sorry`.

## File structure

Place new declarations under a clearly delimited section:

```lean
/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — <TopicName> -/
```

- Use `private` for helpers with no downstream use outside this file.
- Use `theorem` / `lemma` (non-private) for steps that other files will import.
- Add a one-line docstring to each non-private declaration explaining why it is project-local.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any existing declaration. You may add new declarations freely. You must not rename, re-type, or modify signatures of protected declarations.

## API alignment

Mathlib names and signatures are the most common failure source.

- Verify every name with `lean_local_search` or `lean_loogle` — do not rely on memory.
- Check that typeclass assumptions in your statement match what Mathlib requires.
- For `def`-backed types: Lean may not unfold them for typeclass synthesis; use explicit coercions or `change` / `show` in the proof body.

## Write permissions

Only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror the `.lean` path):

```markdown
# Algebra/MathlibSupplement.lean

## Session summary
- Built axiom-clean: `Foo.bar`, `Foo.baz` (lines 12–40)
- Blocked on: `Foo.qux` — needs `Bar.aux` which requires [precise statement]

## Foo.bar (line 12)
- **Approach:** Direct from `Mathlib.Algebra.X` via `simp [Y]`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean

## Foo.qux (not added)
- **Approach 1:** Tried [route] — FAILED because [reason]
- **Approach 2:** Tried informal agent — suggested [sketch], formalization blocked on [specific gap]
- **Next step:** Build `Bar.aux : [precise type statement]` first
- **Dead end:** Do not retry [route] — [why it fails]
```

One section per declaration attempted. For each: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / NOT ADDED), dead-end warnings, next step.

## End-of-session handoff

**Write a `## Summary` section** before the verdicts: declarations added (count + names), declarations blocked (count + why), sorry count before → after across your file.

**Write a `## Needs blueprint entry` section.** This mode is the biggest source of blueprint debt: every non-private definition/lemma you add is, by construction, infrastructure with **no blueprint block yet** — a `lean_aux` node invisible to the dependency graph (`archon dag-query unmatched` will list it) until the planner/reviewer blueprints it. List each new non-private declaration (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) so the 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence the dag agent built is restored next iter. Closing 0 project sorries while adding 3 unreported helpers does not advance the project — it quietly re-introduces the isolation the DAG phase worked to eliminate. Reporting them is mandatory.

**Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest. Only axiom-clean Lean declarations count as progress. Comments, sketches, and log prose do NOT. If you wrote down approaches you didn't attempt, say so:

- `Real progress`: N axiom-clean declarations added — name each one with its line number.
- `Partial progress`: added some declarations, hit a specific blocker on the next — name the blocker precisely (not "it's complex", but the exact type or missing ingredient).
- `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt them — name each. This is valid but weak; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
- `Blocked — alternatives exhausted`: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), alternative routes tried, all failed — name what was tried, why each failed, and the exact type statement of the next needed ingredient.
- `Infrastructure already exists`: Mathlib has what was needed — cite the exact lemma name and namespace.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.FlatBaseChange
8	
9	/-!
10	# Flat base change for the pushforward, global (`H⁰`-as-equalizer) chain
11	
12	This file builds the global ("FBC-B") leg of the `i = 0` flat-base-change package:
13	`H⁰(X, F) = Γ(X, F)` of a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme is the equalizer of
14	a *finite* affine cover, and flat base change commutes with that finite equalizer.
15	
16	It is the companion of `AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.FlatBaseChange`, which it imports
17	read-only (using the affine global-sections comparison as a per-term black box).
18	
19	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex` (FBC-B section).
20	-/
21	
22	universe u
23	
24	open CategoryTheory Limits
25	
26	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
27	
28	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — finite affine covers with quasi-compact overlaps -/
29	
30	/-- A quasi-compact scheme admits a *finite* affine open cover; when it is moreover
31	quasi-separated, every pairwise intersection of cover members is quasi-compact.
32	
33	Project-local: packages `isCompact_iff_finite_and_eq_biUnion_affineOpens` (finite affine
34	subcover of `⊤`) with `quasiSeparatedSpace_iff_forall_affineOpens` (quasi-compact overlaps)
35	into the single combinatorial input feeding the finite sheaf-condition equalizer of the
36	`H⁰` flat-base-change argument. -/
37	theorem Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact (X : Scheme.{u})
38	    [CompactSpace X] [QuasiSeparatedSpace X] :
39	    ∃ s : Set X.affineOpens, s.Finite ∧ (⨆ i ∈ s, (i : X.Opens)) = ⊤ ∧
40	      ∀ U ∈ s, ∀ V ∈ s, IsCompact ((U : Set X) ∩ (V : Set X)) := by
41	  obtain ⟨s, hs, he⟩ :=
42	    (isCompact_iff_finite_and_eq_biUnion_affineOpens (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mp
43	      (by simpa using isCompact_univ (X := ↥X))
44	  refine ⟨s, hs, he.symm, ?_⟩
45	  intro U _ V _
46	  exact quasiSeparatedSpace_iff_forall_affineOpens.mp ‹_› U V
47	
48	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the global sections as a sheaf-condition equalizer -/
49	
50	open TopCat.Presheaf SheafConditionEqualizerProducts in
51	/-- For `M : X.Modules` and any open cover `U : ι → X.Opens`, the sheaf-condition fork
52	of the underlying abelian-group presheaf of `M` is a limit (an equalizer of products):
53	```
54	Γ(M, ⨆ i, U i) ⟶ ∏ i, Γ(M, U i) ⇉ ∏ (i,j), Γ(M, U i ⊓ U j).
55	```
56	This is the equalizer-products form of the sheaf condition specialised to the abelian
57	presheaf `M.presheaf` of a sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules. Combined with the finite affine cover
58	of `Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact` it computes `Γ(X, M) = Γ(M, ⊤)`
59	as a *finite* equalizer; that finiteness is what the flat-base-change argument needs to
60	commute `- ⊗_A B` past the equalizer.
61	
62	Project-local: packages `M.isSheaf` through Mathlib's
63	`isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts` at the level of `X.Modules`. -/
64	noncomputable def Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules)
65	    {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
66	    IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U) :=
67	  ((isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts M.presheaf).mp M.isSheaf U).some
68	
69	open TopCat.Presheaf SheafConditionEqualizerProducts in
70	/-- Consolidation of the two preceding supplements: for a quasi-compact, quasi-separated
71	scheme `X` and `M : X.Modules`, there is a *finite* affine open cover `U : ι → X.Opens`
72	(`ι` finite, each `U i` affine, `⨆ i, U i = ⊤`, all pairwise intersections quasi-compact)
73	for which `Γ(X, M) = Γ(M, ⊤)` is the equalizer of the finite sheaf-condition fork of `U`.
74	
75	Project-local: combines `Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact` (L1) with
76	`Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork` (L2) into the directly-usable "global sections as a
77	finite equalizer" input of the flat-base-change argument. -/
78	theorem Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork (X : Scheme.{u})
79	    [CompactSpace X] [QuasiSeparatedSpace X] (M : X.Modules) :
80	    ∃ (ι : Type u) (_ : Finite ι) (U : ι → X.Opens),
81	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ (⨆ i, U i = ⊤) ∧
82	      (∀ i j, IsCompact ((U i : Set X) ∩ (U j : Set X))) ∧
83	      Nonempty (IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U)) := by
84	  obtain ⟨s, hs, hcov, hqc⟩ := X.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact
85	  have hfin : Finite ↥s := hs.to_subtype
86	  refine ⟨↥s, hfin, fun i => ((i : X.affineOpens) : X.Opens), fun i => i.1.2, ?_, ?_,
87	    ⟨Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork M _⟩⟩
88	  · rw [← hcov, iSup_subtype']
89	  · rintro ⟨i, hi⟩ ⟨j, hj⟩
90	    exact hqc i hi j hj
91	
92	end AlgebraicGeometry
